nameMeanings.name: Swynford
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Gloucestershire, Oxfordshire, Kent): habitational name from Old Swinford (Worcestershire), Kingswinford (Staffordshire), Swinford (Leicestershire), Swinford in Cumnor (Berkshire), or Swinford in Hothfield (Kent), all of which are recorded Swineford or Swyneford in medieval documents. The placenames derive from Old English swīn ‘swine, pig’ + ford ‘ford’.

variants.name: Swineford
meanings: Scottish: habitational name from one of the English places that gave rise to   Swinford  .
Americanized form of German Schweinfurt(h), a habitational name from a place so named (Franconia).

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

en.lifeSummary: Robert de Swynford was born in 1285, in Lincolnshire, England as the son of William de Swineford. He married Margaret De Kettelthorpe. They were the parents of at least 2 sons. He died in 1345, in Parts of Lindsey, Lincolnshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 60.
